More Notes: Each additional dry spell day beyond 28 days that occurs during the historically hottest part of the year is associated with approximately 0.1 percentage point lower growth in the FAO CPI. Data spans 1979-2016.
sourcematerial: Each additional dry spell day beyond 28 days that occurs during the historically hottest part of the year is associated with approximately 0.1 percentage point lower growth in the FAO CPI (Table 3). Because we condition on drought, this effect is best interpreted as a direct effect of dry spells.

More Notes: Correlation between dry spell exposure and crop production growth under drought conditions (SPEI ≤ 0). 1979-2016 average.
Correlation between dry spell exposure and crop production growth under non-drought conditions (SPEI > 0). 1979-2016 average.
Correlation between dry spell exposure and crop production growth under high aridity (Aridity Index below median). 1979-2016 average.
Correlation between dry spell exposure and crop production growth under low aridity (Aridity Index above median). 1979-2016 average.
Based on 21+ day dry spells. p<0.1. The middle of the 1979-2016 period is roughly 2000.

More Notes: Dry spell day effect when aridity index <= 0.65, area-weighted.
sourcematerial: Using a global panel of daily precipitation and temperature from 1979 to 2016, we provide evidence that 4-5 week dry spells that occur during the hottest part of the year depress growth in aggregate crop production.; Page 8: "It also considers effect heterogeneity by constructing variables based on drought, aridity, and irrigation."; Page 16: "The analysis uses a series of alternative specifications to examine the robustness of dry spell estimates, including adjusting for lagged agricultural output, dry spell, and heat wave effects."; Page 38: "The analysis involves estimating the heterogeneous effects of dry spells on CPI and cereal yield, depending on aridity, drought, and irrigation, using area-weighted models and equations (3-5)."; Page 39: "Country-level analyses assess heterogeneity with split-sample and interaction-term approaches to understand effects of dry spells based on aridity and drought."; Table 5 in the source lists various impacts of dry spells on CPI and cereal yield, segregated by factors such as aridity, drought conditions, and irrigation presence.

More Notes: Dry spell day effect when aridity index > 0.65, area-weighted.

Table A2: Results from distributed lag model estimation

Dry spell day 0.0011** 0.0010* 0.0009 0.0009 0.0007
 (1-year lag) (0.0006) (0.0006) (0.0006) (0.0007) (0.0008)
Notes: Estimates from variants of equation (1) in the main text in which all environmental variables (dry
spell days, heat wave days, and drought, as well as temperature, precipitation, and their squares) enter not
only as current-year variables, but also in lagged form from 1 to 5 years. Columns (1)-(5) in the table
successively introduce additional lags. Estimates reflect the effect of the given lag of an additional dry
spell day in a specification with 28+ day dry spells and 9+ day heat waves. Standard
errors are clustered at the country level. ***: p<0.01, **: p<0.05, *: p<0.1.

More Notes: One-year lag effect from column (1). Significantly positive at p<0.1. This represents the effect during the sample, with the midpoint year (1979-2016)

sourcematerial:
Using a global panel of daily precipitation and temperature from 1979 to 2016, we provide evidence that 4-5 week dry spells that occur during the hottest part of the year depress growth in aggregate crop production. ... (Page 2)

Estimations use sequential g-estimation to account for the potential influence of dry spells on the 12-month SPEI in a given year. Numbers in parentheses indicate 95% confidence intervals estimated via panel bootstrap. ... Losses in crop yields due to dry spells are concentrated in areas without irrigation, indicating a potential role for adaptation. (Pages 44-45)

The paper discusses drought as a driver of risk, using the Standardized Precipitation-Evaporation Index (SPEI) to evaluate exposure to drought conditions. (Page 9)

Accumulated impacts of persistent dry spells in grid cells where maximum temperature coincides with low precipitation levels are weighted according to crop area (MIRCA2000 dataset) and measured against temperature-crop growth correlations. (Page 44-45)

Estimates from Equation (2) of effects of dry spell days alone (main effect; 𝛼1), heat wave days alone (main effect; 𝛼2), ... dry spell effects are statistically significant at p < 0.05 for SPEI = -2 and SPEI = -1. (Pages 37, 47)

Standard errors are clustered at the country level. ***: p<0.01, **: p<0.05, *: p<0.1." (Page 45)

More Notes: Total effect of an additional dry spell day at or above the specified minimum duration among observations specified by the column header; using median country value of the aridity index which is 0.997.
sourcematerial: Total effect of an additional dry spell day at or above the specified minimum duration among observations specified by the column header. Each specification also includes the number of heat wave days using a minimum heat wave duration of 9 days. Aridity split sample and interaction term models use the median country value of the aridity index, which is 0.997. The top row of estimates uses split-sample estimation, allowing the effects of all covariates to differ by subsample, while the bottom row allows only the effects of dry spells to differ across groups of observations. In both cases, we use country-level aridity and SPEI.  ***: p<0.01, **: p<0.05, *: p<0.1.
